Author: Michal Migurski
Title: Weeknotes, 2026W07: Two Little Updates
Text:
Weeknotes, 2026W07: Two Little Updates
I’ve been making steady progress on PlanScore and Boundary Issues. In both cases I’m working on familiar (to me) systems and codebases, and recording ways that LLM assistants do or don’t help. My coding agent profile generally asks the machine to always record my verbatim instructions so I can review the patterns that work better than others.
Boundary Issues
The project is a stab at what an open and shared workflow might look like for political points of view driven from OpenStreetMap data. Back when we were doing OpenAddresses more actively (e.g. 2014, 2014 again, and 2015) we set up a continuous integration service to watch for changes to pull requests and add comments to the discussion with generated screenshots of how the data would look once integrated. For a volunteer-driven effort it’s important to provide acknowledgement so contributors know if their stuff works.
I’ve been doing much the same for Boundary Issues, with a goal of adding an automated check to each PR. No pictures yet, but the integration with Github is functioning correctly:
Stuff That Didn’t Work
When implementing this, my initial thought was to borrow from Drew’s Whenwords project to do a “no-code” spec for the function that responds to PRs so that I could benefit from the 100x faster cold start times of compiled C++ code in AWS Lambda without actually knowing or caring to learn C++. This did sort-of work but was incredibly temperamental: sometimes the LLM successfully found and linked against the AWS C++ CDK, other times it ran itself ragged trying to integrate. Coding and build times were many minutes in length. I did not find an elegant way to run it headlessly inside a Makefile. I discarded this idea pretty quickly and resigned myself to waiting 2000 or more milliseconds for Python programs to start.
Stuff That Worked
I love AWS Step Functions, a state machine service we used heavily at Felt, and I’m using it here. In my past work with this service (and integration with Github Actions) the coding and debugging loop always depends on viewing AWS logs to validate progress. I got to be really skilled and fast at this, but it made the work hard to pick up for other colleagues who didn’t have my muscle memory. Here I provided the LLM with keys to AWS and to Github and guessed that the ubiquity of these services in training data would let my coding agent figure out the right curl
and aws
calls to observe success. This worked really well: API docs for these services are stable and established, and in most cases the right AWS logs were easy for the machine to find, parse, and act on.
When defining AWS resources, I generally like the Python implementation of CDK but in this case I wanted to see if it was easy for a machine to define and test Cloudformation YAML and save me a bit of boilerplate. This turned out to be a success also; these services have been stable for years and AWS tends to support a “one-way door” policy with its APIs that keeps them supported forever. Issuing prompts with direct references to AWS services and funky local branch setups supported by scripts tended to yield the results I wanted. Example prompt:
We are working in two branches here, and coordinating changes between the two of them in order to test changes in Github Actions. The basic setup is already complete in ../.github/workflows, and you have the permissions you need for AWS in ~/.aws and Github in ../.env. There’s a script for pushing to origin in push-it.sh you may want to consult that shows how the branch migurski/do-not-merge (PR #4) is stacked atop the branch migurski/execute-a-state-machine where we are actually working. Our goal is to expand the existing CloudFormation definition to include a state machine, to have the lambda_handler() in index.py execute that state machine with its payload, and to observe that this is successful by looking at logs from Github and from AWS. The state machine should for now be just a single step, running a Docker image Lambda function based on ./Dockerfile that can check out the HEAD of this repo to test that a pull request worked.
My next step with this is to make it interact with PRs beyond just a green check. An interesting challenge with this specific data and our OpenStreetMap upstream source is that slowness might be desirable: I’m considering add a one-week delay into the middle of the process so it checks user input twice: once immediately to validate that the interpretation of OSM data works, and a second time later just in case a sensitive border object has been modified again. Borders can be subject to edit wars and we can use some of the long, cheap delay features in AWS Step Functions to allow only stable changes to be included.
PlanScore
For years, score pages on PlanScore have been static. They’re designed to work as PDF attachments to legal briefs so this is intentional, but in this year’s mid-cycle redistricting we are keeping an eye on changes specific to the 2026 mid-term election. We’re expecting a major leftward shift in voting patterns compared to 2024, maybe a return to 2020-style patterns as Hispanic voters change their preferences, and each week brings news of changes to incumbents seeking seats. PlanScore pages will make it possible to adjust these values even as the site and service stays built on AWS S3.
We’re now calculating a multi-dimensional matrix of predictions and exposing views of it, with a re-write of the core scoring code that uses fast, efficient NumPy arrays and calculations over many simultaneous scenarios.
Stuff That Worked
I’ve been pleased with the LLM’s ability to act on biggish goals, and I’ve been able to express my initial prompts at a high level with good feedback from the planner to let me refine directions. This was my initial instruction in a sequence of work, plus a followup once I saw the plan and realized I hadn’t described something correctly:
“We are going to vectorize some low-level functions in planscore/score.py, starting with swing_vote(). The new vectorized_swing() function should accept a three-dimensional ndarray whose three axes are district, simulation, and party. The last axis is length=2 and by convention the blue party is always at 0 and the red party is always at 1. We will need a dedicated test method for this in planscore/tests/test_score.py and we should start there. It should cover the same cases as test_swing_vote() with one addition: sometimes the district vote total can be zero and we want to be resilient to that. The return value of the new fuction should be a one-dimensional array with length equal to the number of simulations. Tell me your plan.”
“I made a mistake. The return value should be an array with the same dimensionality as the input array, with new votes in the cells. Please update your plan.”
It’s also pleasantly skilled at debugging with high-level guidance, though in this case I got lucky that I noticed the wrong output in a subtle part of the interface I might’ve overlooked. This honestly freaks me out a little because sweating these particular details is important for PlanScore’s audience, and prompting loops are not a great fit for detail-orientation:
“I am comparing the results of running `planscore-score-locally https://planscore--dev.s3.amazonaws.com/uploads/20260127T042256.286915550Z/index.json` on main vs. this branch. Mostly the numbers all match as I expect, but I see that the output says “GK Bias: -13.7%; 13% favor D” here and “GK Bias: -13.7%; 9% favor D” on main. Explain the discrepancy and tell me a plan to fix it, feel free to use git checkout to switch branches.”
“Can you recommend an addition to the tests that ensures this fix continues to work? Also determine if it needs to be applied to any of the other new vectorized functions.”
Stuff That Didn’t Work
I really had to hold the LLM’s hand through this process. Ultimately it was successful but I routinely found it cutting corners like mostly vectorizing a piece of code but still leaving a plain old iteration around. I’m in-for-a-penny-in-for-a-pound on NumPy here, I want to be confident that the LLM is able to understand what “vectorize” means and apply it without falling back to a mix of control loops.
“Next step: we want to add a vectorized_PB() just like calculate_PB() with its tests. The input should be a three-dimensional (districts, simulations, parties) ndarray with identical conventions to vectorize_swing(). The output should be a one-dimensional (simulations) ndarray with the calculated partisan bias score for each simulation. The tests should replicate what we’re checking in test_calculate_PB(). Tell me your plan.”
“I don’t like the iteration over simulations in the new function, can you think of a way to do more of the logic outside that loop while still passing tests”
Another place where I needed to intervene was planning for future flexibility and additional array dimensions. I didn’t find this surprising and expected to have to coach the LLM through negative indexing.
Next
Both of these projects are in-development and I’ll post about publicly visible progress.
